title: Check if a Graph is Bipartite
description: Given a graph represented as an adjacency list, determine if it is possible to color the graph using two colors such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color. A graph is bipartite if and only if it does not contain an odd-length cycle.

examples.explanation: The graph can be colored with two colors: 0 and 1. Vertex 0 can be colored 0, vertex 1 can be colored 1, vertex 2 can be colored 0, and vertex 3 can be colored 1. No two adjacent vertices have the same color.
The graph contains an odd-length cycle (0-1-2-0). Therefore, it is impossible to color the graph with two colors without having two adjacent nodes of the same color.
An empty graph is considered bipartite because there are no edges, hence no adjacent vertices to conflict the coloring.

constraints: The number of vertices in the graph will be between 1 and 100.
The graph is represented as a list of lists, where each sublist contains the indices of its neighbors.
hints: Consider using a breadth-first search or depth-first search to traverse the graph.
Maintain a color array to keep track of the colors assigned to each vertex.
Check for conflicts when coloring adjacent nodes during traversal.
If you find an uncolored node, start a new traversal from there to cover all disconnected components.
